```{=latex}
\ifdefined\ArchTwoReviewBuild
\mainmatter
\fi
```

# The Architecture 2.0 Moonshot {#sec-moonshot}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The purpose of computing is insight, not numbers."*
>
> — Richard Hamming, *Numerical Methods for Scientists and Engineers* (1962) [@Hamming1962NumericalMethods]
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Richard Hamming, a pioneer in computer science, argued that
computing should yield insight rather than raw data. The distinction matters
here. AI-assisted architecture should help architects ask better questions,
test alternatives, explain mechanisms, and defend decisions. Producing more
candidates is useful only when it improves that work.
:::

::: {.callout-crux}
What capabilities must an AI-assisted architecture process combine to turn
system intent into an efficient system design that an architect can defend
with evidence?
:::

For roughly fifty years, processor generations improved through a familiar progression where device scaling regularly delivered faster, cheaper, and more energy-efficient transistors. Quantitative analysis gave architects a way to compare the resulting design choices. As scaling slowed, microarchitecture, parallelism, and eventually domain specialization provided the remaining gains [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. This shift is visible in fifty years of frontier microprocessor data. Clock frequency and typical power flattened in the mid-2000s, while transistor counts continued to rise and core counts turned upward (@fig-microprocessor-trends).

```{python}
#| label: fig-microprocessor-trends
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021.** Data from Karl Rupp's public microprocessor dataset are summarized as the highest value in each four-year interval [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. Clock frequency and typical power flatten in the mid-2000s as Dennard scaling ends, while transistor counts continue to rise and core counts turn upward [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon]. The figure shows frontier values rather than every processor; the era boundaries are approximate [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-scale line chart of frontier microprocessor trends from 1971 to 2021 with five series. Transistors per chip rise from a few thousand to tens of billions by 2021. Single-thread performance rises steeply until the mid-2000s and then slows. Clock frequency plateaus near 3 to 5 GHz and typical power near 100 to 500 W after about 2005, while logical core counts climb from one to the hundreds. Dashed vertical lines mark the approximate end of Dennard scaling around 2005 and the specialization turn around 2016. A dotted boundary past 2025 marks AI-assisted compositional design as a prospective era rather than a measured trend."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style

apply_style()

# Fifty-year microprocessor trend data; per-series frontier points
# (per-4-year-bucket maxima) transcribed from the public dataset
# github.com/karlrupp/microprocessor-trend-data (50yrs/*.dat, CC-BY 4.0),
# which compiles the Horowitz/Labonte/Shacham/Olukotun/Hammond/Batten
# collection through 2010 plus Rupp's additions through 2021. Values are
# dataset-transcribed, not recomputed; fractional years as in the dataset;
# transistors in thousands, single-thread performance in SpecINT x 1000.
# Era-boundary annotations (~2005 Dennard end, ~2016 specialization turn)
# are approximate: [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; # @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Accessed 2026-07-14.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-micro-trend.csv
series = [
    {
        "label": "Transistors\n(thousands)",
        "color": COLORS["purple"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 6.1), (1982.3, 136), (1985.9, 274), (1989.5, 1210),
                   (1992.7, 3110), (1998.9, 15300), (2002.9, 221000),
                   (2007.0, 583000), (2009.7, 2.31e6), (2014.8, 5.7e6),
                   (2018.5, 2.11e7), (2021.9, 5.82e7)],
        "label_y": 5.82e7,
    },
    {
        "label": "Single-thread perf\n(SpecINT x 1000)",
        "color": COLORS["blue"],
        "points": [(1988.7, 83.5), (1994.8, 599), (1998.9, 3550),
                   (2002.4, 9390), (2007.0, 35200), (2010.3, 42000),
                   (2014.8, 66000), (2017.6, 83600), (2021.3, 105000)],
        "label_y": 1.05e5,
    },
    {
        "label": "Frequency (MHz)",
        "color": COLORS["orange"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 2.02), (1982.3, 6.1), (1985.9, 16.1),
                   (1988.7, 40.3), (1994.7, 297), (1997.5, 538),
                   (2002.4, 2250), (2004.9, 3650), (2007.7, 4660),
                   (2012.7, 3700), (2016.7, 4000), (2020.9, 3200)],
        "label_y": 3.2e3,
    },
    {
        "label": "Typical power (W)",
        "color": COLORS["red"],
        "points": [(1974.3, 0.922), (1982.3, 3.02), (1986.2, 3.02),
                   (1988.7, 3.96), (1993.0, 35.2), (1998.9, 90.6),
                   (2001.7, 98.2), (2003.4, 155), (2010.4, 168),
                   (2013.4, 300), (2018.5, 250), (2021.9, 500)],
        "label_y": 7.5e2,
    },
    {
        "label": "Logical cores",
        "color": COLORS["green"],
        "points": [(1971.9, 1), (1979.6, 1), (1985.9, 1), (1988.7, 1),
                   (1992.4, 1), (1995.4, 1), (1999.2, 1), (2006.0, 8),
                   (2010.2, 16), (2013.4, 240), (2016.5, 272), (2021.9, 208)],
        "label_y": 1.1e2,
    },
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 3.6))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.075, right=0.775, top=0.90, bottom=0.225)

ax.set_yscale("log")
ax.set_xlim(1970, 2032.5)
ax.set_ylim(0.4, 3e9)

for x in (2005, 2016):
    ax.axvline(x, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
               linestyle=(0, (4, 3)), zorder=1)

era_labels = [
    (1987.5, "Device-scaling era"),
    (2010.5, "Parallelism era"),
    (2020.5, "Specialization\nera"),
]
for x, text in era_labels:
    ax.text(x, 6.5e8, text, ha="center", va="center", fontsize=6.2,
            fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"])

# Prospective era: annotation only, no data points and no band fill, styled
# like the other era boundaries but dotted to mark a prospective argument.
ax.axvline(2025, color=COLORS["muted"], linewidth=0.8,
           linestyle=(0, (1.5, 2.5)), zorder=1)
ax.text(2028.9, 6.5e8, "AI-assisted\ncompositional\ndesign?", ha="center",
        va="center", fontsize=5.4, fontweight="bold",
        color=COLORS["workload_ink"], linespacing=1.2)
ax.text(2028.9, 2.5e7, "(prospective,\nnot measured\ndata)", ha="center",
        va="top", fontsize=5.4, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.25)

ax.text(2004.3, 4e7, "Dennard scaling\nends", ha="right", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)
ax.text(2016.4, 1.6e6, "specialization\nturn", ha="left", va="center",
        fontsize=5.6, color=COLORS["muted"], linespacing=1.15)

for s in series:
    xs = [p[0] for p in s["points"]]
    ys = [p[1] for p in s["points"]]
    ax.plot(xs, ys, color=s["color"], linewidth=1.6, zorder=2)
    ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=13, color=s["color"], zorder=3)
    ax.text(1.015, s["label_y"], s["label"], ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.0, fontweight="bold", color=s["color"],
            clip_on=False, linespacing=1.15,
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform())

ax.set_xticks([1970, 1980, 1990, 2000, 2010, 2020])
ax.set_yticks([1, 1e2, 1e4, 1e6, 1e8])
ax.set_yticklabels([r"$10^0$", r"$10^2$", r"$10^4$", r"$10^6$", r"$10^8$"])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.6)

for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Four-year frontier maxima from Rupp's 50-year dataset; "
    "era boundaries are approximate.",
    xywh=(0.075, 0.03, 0.70, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.6,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

@fig-microprocessor-trends marks the end of a free lunch [@Sutter2005FreeLunch], exposing the harsh economics of the power/performance wall. Transistor counts kept climbing, but once Dennard scaling ended and typical power flattened around 2005, the single-thread gains that had arrived automatically each generation stopped. The field recovered them first through parallelism and then through specialization. Each turn handed architects more of the machine to manage rather than inheriting it from the next process node. The end of Dennard scaling demands a structural shift, not just faster versions of old tools. The figure's dotted rightmost era marks a prospective next turn: one where AI helps us co-design across those layers rather than tune each in isolation.

That shift multiplied the architectures, software interfaces, and physical constraints we must hold in view. New dataflows, memory systems, and programming interfaces appeared in quick succession [@HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge]. Yet much of the coordination among workloads, compilers, simulators, RTL, and physical design still falls directly to architects.

Specialization also made architecture decisions tightly coupled. A new dataflow can simultaneously change memory traffic, compiler mapping, verification effort, and physical feasibility. Generating RTL is only 20% of the cost; the remaining 80% is functional verification. If an AI generates thousands of novel accelerators a day but humans must manually write UVM testbenches for them, it hasn't solved the bottleneck—it has just created a catastrophic verification traffic jam. Crucially, the hardest bottleneck is often the software stack: a novel spatial accelerator is dead silicon if the compiler cannot be co-adapted to target it. As these cross-layer dependencies multiply, the resulting design space becomes a massive, non-convex[^fn-non-convex-c01] combinatorial space that human intuition can no longer navigate reliably.

Worse, navigating this space carries brutal economic stakes. In software engineering, an AI hallucination can be hotfixed in seconds. In hardware, an AI hallucination that violates a physical DRC constraint or thermal limit can cause a catastrophic silicon failure, leading to a $50 million mask respin and a 12-month delay. The cost of a mistake forces an architect to coordinate far more models, tools, and evidence just to defend a single choice.

[^fn-non-convex-c01]: **Non-convex search space**: A search space with many local optima, where stepwise improvement can strand a search far from the best design. Greedy and gradient-style methods carry no optimality guarantee in such a space, which is why scale defeats both exhaustive sweeps and human intuition.

This pressure does not make AI strictly necessary, but it explains the turn toward exploring agentic AI assistance. The golden age established that specialization is now where the major gains live. Architecture 2.0 asks whether AI can help us design those specialized systems under brutal cross-layer constraints. Doing so shifts the unit of work from the finished artifact to the reviewable study that produced it. The human architect remains the final judge. Whether this help is real, and why the need for it is now pressing, is the subject of @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After reading this chapter, you can:

- Deconstruct high-level natural language design prompts into verifiable constraints spanning the instruction set architecture, microarchitecture, compiler, and physical silicon layers.
- Contrast human-orchestrated Architecture 1.0 design flows with mixed-initiative Architecture 2.0 paradigms regarding decision ownership, explicit state representation, and automated tool integration.
- Evaluate the constraints imposed by data scarcity and the proxy-to-signoff gap when utilizing machine learning surrogate models within electronic design automation verification loops.
- Formulate multi-dimensional efficiency claims that evaluate hardware performance, physical resource budgets, and verification overhead using rejectable evidence standards.

:::

## The AI-Assisted Proposition

The field is now applying AI to the design processes that produced those accelerators, targeting tasks from design-space exploration to physical placement [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE; @MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. In this book, *AI* serves as an umbrella term. It spans traditional machine learning for fast screening, smaller language models (SLMs) for tactical generation, and large language models (LLMs) for broader reasoning. 

However, applying these methods to architecture is fundamentally constrained by the "Dark Data" problem. Unlike software engineering, which can pre-train LLMs on billions of lines of open-source GitHub code, advanced hardware data (like 3nm physical layouts and tape-out telemetry) is fiercely guarded and proprietary. Because offline training data is scarce, AI in architecture must be highly agentic—learning actively by interrogating simulators and EDA tools in the loop. This active learning immediately hits the simulation time-wall. Unlike a software unit test that returns a reward in milliseconds, a cycle-accurate hardware simulator takes hours, and physical sign-off takes days. This brutal reward latency makes brute-force reinforcement learning impossible, forcing a reliance on multi-fidelity escalation. 

This assistance is not free. Large language models add computational overhead, latency, and financial cost. Orchestrating an AI-assisted design loop therefore requires weighing inference costs against the simulator time saved, while ensuring every generated claim is physically grounded.

Consider entering the following design brief into an AI system. We use this to test system intent rather than just benchmark current model capabilities.

> **Lighthouse prompt.** Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3 W TDP target in a 3 nm-class LP mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives.

We call this the *Lighthouse prompt*. No public result reviewed for this book establishes an end-to-end answer. Its scale deliberately exposes the architecture work that a smaller generation task would hide, providing a long-term target for judging partial progress. We choose mobile XR specifically because it forces simultaneous extreme constraints on latency, thermal limits, and energy efficiency. Furthermore, it forces compositional design. An accelerator block in an industrial SoC is not a clean-sheet island; it must interface seamlessly with legacy, black-box IP over rigid standard protocols (like AMBA AXI). The AI's design is dead on arrival if it optimizes PPA but drops an AXI handshake.

This compact prompt is not yet enough to guide real architecture work. We must still define the workload and baseline, choose meaningful alternatives, connect them to executable tools, and decide what evidence could support or overturn a recommendation. While AI may assist with each step, it can also propose candidate designs, scripts, and summaries far faster than we can evaluate them.

The prompt does not imply a single model, single invocation, or uninterrupted agent. A credible answer must combine multiple methods, representations, software components, tools, experiments, and reviews. Our true object of study is the complete AI-assisted architecture process and the decision it supports, rather than the apparent fluency of any single generated response.

If a method proposes a thousand accelerators but we cannot test why they differ, explain why most should be rejected, or state what would overturn the apparent winner, the extra proposals have not advanced the design. A fast analytical model could still help by screening out thermal-budget violations before they consume scarce simulator or physical-design time. But the value lies in reducing expensive engineering work, not in the raw proposal count.

We use this same Lighthouse prompt throughout the book. This lets later chapters change the method or the target stack layer without quietly shifting the workload, constraints, or standard of evidence.

## A Short History of AI in Architecture

Pursuing this prompt builds on a deep existing foundation. AI has a long history inside computer architecture, where researchers have applied it to branch predictors, prefetchers, surrogate models, reinforcement-learning[^fn-reinforcement-learning-c01] controllers, design-space exploration, and autotuning. Chen, Penney, and Jiménez survey this progress in *AI for Computer Architecture: Principles, Practice, and Prospects* [@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture]. Predictive design-space exploration and tensor-program autotuning offer early examples of this integration [@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE; @ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In most of this prior work, learning operates either inside an artifact or performs a specific tactical job within a human-defined architecture workflow. @sec-appendix-d-ai-for-architecture-map groups this prior work into families and maps each to the chapters that develop it.

[^fn-reinforcement-learning-c01]: **Reinforcement learning (RL)**: A machine-learning method in which an agent learns to choose actions that maximize a cumulative reward signal from its environment [@SuttonBarto2018ReinforcementLearning]. In an architecture study it is one search method among several, and its proposals need the same evidence and checks as any other candidate generator.

Recent generative systems are now tackling bounded parts of that workflow, such as proposing architecture configurations, invoking tools, and revising searches based on returned results [@WangEtAl2026AgentDSE]. Domain-adapted language models like ChipNeMo—tuned on internal design data for chip-design assistant tasks—are a concrete instance of this trend [@LiuEtAl2023ChipNeMo]. While these systems can generate text and scripts, those artifacts represent only a fraction of the effort required to connect an architecture question to a final decision. Our scope includes both learned components embedded within a design and AI systems used to conduct the broader engineering work. In either context, the central question remains: how does the method change the evidence supporting an architectural conclusion?

Architecture work has always moved through repeated cycles of analysis and revision. We frame a problem, choose abstractions, construct models, select workloads, explore alternatives, evaluate results, reject weak candidates, and revise our assumptions. This progression moves from intent and workload characterization to architecture decomposition, construction, evaluation, explanation, and finally decision.

Consider weighing a larger L2 cache for a stated workload mix. We might use gem5, a cycle-level architectural simulator, or FireSim, an FPGA-accelerated full-system RTL platform, to sweep cache sizes across representative workloads [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5; @KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]. We compare miss behavior, execution time, and energy estimates before explaining whether locality, contention, or latency hiding drove the outcome. Throughout this process, simulators, compilers, synthesis tools, and design reviews mediate our judgment of whether the evidence justifies a design change.

For generative and tool-using systems, describing each method by the architecture job it performs is clearer than categorizing it by model family. These jobs rarely occur in a fixed order. Generation might expose a missing constraint, while prediction could reveal that the available evidence fails to separate two candidates. Optimization can efficiently allocate scarce simulator or EDA runs, while critique and verification can disqualify an apparent winner. Because repair modifies the artifact, it may invalidate earlier measurements.

To evaluate any of these AI contributions, we must be able to see exactly what the system inspected and changed, how it obtained feedback, which observations counted as evidence, and which checks could reject its result. 

This introduces a profound epistemological shift for the human architect. In Architecture 1.0, the architect was primarily a *creator*, manually writing RTL and scripts. In Architecture 2.0, because an AI can generate thousands of candidates in a day, the architect's role shifts to that of an adversarial *interrogator*. The human acts as the ultimate skeptical judge—designing rejection checks, demanding causal proofs, and fighting the AI's tendency to "reward hack" simulators for artificially perfect results. For example, if an AI is tasked to minimize power, it will aggressively share unisolated hardware resources, inadvertently reinventing side-channel vulnerabilities like Spectre unless formally constrained. While the degree of automation can vary, automating a step never transfers responsibility for the final architectural conclusion.

While embedded ML optimizes run-time behavior by living inside the artifact, our focus moving forward is on AI as an agent of design automation, steering the engineering workflow itself. AI-assisted architecture extends a long history of design automation, but it begins from a broader statement of intent than earlier tools.

@fig-automation-ladder places this broader form of AI participation in the history of design automation. The dates are approximate, as each stage accepts a broader statement of intent and produces a larger artifact.

1. **1980s--1990s (Logic Synthesis):** Logic synthesis translated Boolean and RTL descriptions into gate-level structure under library, physical, and timing constraints [@DeMicheli1994SynthesisOptimization].
2. **1990s--2000s (High-Level Synthesis):** High-level synthesis generated RTL candidates from algorithmic descriptions and moved part of the tactical microarchitecture search into tools [@CoussyMorawiec2008HighLevelSynthesis].
3. **2000s--2010s (Domain-Specific Synthesis):** Building on deductive program synthesis from the 1970s, domain-specific synthesis generated executable code from specifications constrained by domain-specific languages [@GulwaniPolozovSingh2017ProgramSynthesis].
4. **2010s--2020s (Agile Hardware Generation):** Agile hardware generation used parameterized templates, such as Chisel and its FIRRTL intermediate representation, to create specialized SoC instances from reusable IP [@chipyard].
5. **Prospective direction (AI-Assisted Architecture):** AI may help connect workload intent, software contracts, architectural alternatives, executable tools, experiments, and physical constraints inside a scoped study. The research question is whether the result can become a finding or decision supported by evidence, even when much of the study remains under human control.

![**Four historical stages and one prospective direction.** The historical stages accept progressively broader statements of intent and produce larger artifacts. AI-assisted architecture is shown as a research direction toward a scoped study that yields a finding or decision supported by evidence, not as an inevitable next stage. Era spans are approximate and follow the literature cited above.](images/F2b-automation-ladder-timeline){#fig-automation-ladder width="85%" fig-alt="Staircase diagram with four historical design-automation stages and a prospective AI-assisted architecture direction. From bottom to top: logic synthesis, high-level synthesis, domain-specific synthesis, agile hardware generation, and AI-assisted architecture. A horizontal arrow marks approximate time order and a vertical arrow marks the widening unit of intent and artifact."}

These earlier stages remain essential. Logic synthesis became dependable only because static timing analysis and equivalence checking could reliably expose bad netlists. AI-assisted architecture draws on these established tools while connecting decisions made across stages. The evidence must identify which tool produced each result and which assumptions limit how that result can be used.

Reviewable evidence from iterative work should include the proposals
considered, the results obtained, the candidates rejected or revised, and the
decisions that followed. Different readers use this information for different
reasons.

- **Researcher:** Describe an AI-for-architecture paper by naming the
  architecture question, the role AI played, the artifact or finding produced,
  and the evidence and limits behind it.
- **Tool builder:** Ask whether a harness keeps the inputs, settings,
  results, and failures that another method or engineer would need.
- **Author or reviewer:** Ask what would reject the claim, why the measured
  mechanism supports it, and which decision it can justify.

## Transitioning from Architecture 1.0 to 2.0 {#sec-from-architecture-10-to-architecture-20}

These review questions apply whether we orchestrate every step or AI participates in part of the work. Architecture 1.0 is shorthand for familiar, human-orchestrated design: we define the problem, choose models, run tools, interpret feedback, and decide what to build. Architecture 2.0 retains that accountability but lets AI shoulder more of the execution burden [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. It asserts that our true value lies in orchestration, not manual implementation, as summarized in @tbl-arch1-vs-arch2.

| **Feature** | **Architecture 1.0** | **Architecture 2.0** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Orchestration** | Fully human-driven | AI-assisted and mixed-initiative |
| **Exploration** | Manual sweeps over small parameter spaces | Generative methods that draw on represented architectural knowledge to propose novel designs |
| **Tool Execution** | Scripts and manual toolchain invocation | A tool harness that drives brittle EDA and simulation flows through typed interfaces and reads back structured feedback, including the failures a reviewer must see |
| **Decision Making** | Solely human insight | AI rationale paired with human final judgment |

: **Architecture 1.0 vs Architecture 2.0.** Moving from manual orchestration to AI-assisted workflows while retaining human accountability. {#tbl-arch1-vs-arch2}

AI may help us interpret an initial request, characterize workload distributions, propose an architecture decomposition, construct artifacts, select evaluations, diagnose results, and prepare a recommendation. These represent possible forms of assistance across several methods, not a claim that any single public system performs the complete workflow today. We organize this participation into six activities: formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions. While producing a plausible artifact helps, it does not replace the hard work of intent framing, workload characterization, evaluation, and decision-making.

As AI takes on more of this load, our evidence must explicitly identify the inputs and artifact versions available to the system, the actions it took, the feedback it received, and the candidates it rejected. Documenting this trail lets other architects reconstruct what happened and determine exactly what the result supports.

During our XR subsystem exploration, for example, a method might propose vector-lane configurations, a surrogate[^fn-surrogate-model-c01] could screen them for 3 W thermal violations, and a critic could flag unsupported compiler intrinsics. The final contribution might be a novel cache design, an architectural finding, a reusable method, or a decision supported by the available evidence.

[^fn-surrogate-model-c01]: **Surrogate model**: A cheap, data-driven stand-in for an expensive simulator that lets a method screen many candidates quickly. Its score is only a proxy, so a promising candidate still needs a higher-fidelity check before it can support a design decision.

@fig-design-loop-breaks shows the practical difference. Both sides iterate, and both depend on architectural judgment. The AI-assisted side makes the inputs, artifact versions, permitted actions, tool feedback, evidence, rejected candidates, and decision authority explicit enough for a system and its reviewers to use.

![**AI participation makes design state explicit.** Both loops iterate and both rest on architectural judgment, but the human-orchestrated study on the left keeps most of that state in the experts' heads, while the AI-assisted study on the right represents it where a system and its reviewers can inspect it.](images/F2a-design-loop-breaks){#fig-design-loop-breaks width="85%" fig-alt="Two side-by-side loop diagrams compare human-orchestrated architecture work, where experts carry much of the study state, with an AI-assisted project that represents inputs, actions, evidence, rejection, and decision authority explicitly."}

An AI system that calls a simulator, writes RTL, or summarizes papers still depends on sound systems engineering. The machine learning field has long grappled with the "Sim-to-Real gap," where agents that succeed in simulation fail in the real world [@TobinEtAl2017DomainRandomization]. In ML-driven hardware design, this manifests as the "Proxy-to-Signoff gap," where fast ML surrogate models (like GNNs for congestion prediction) diverge from ground-truth EDA signoff tools. In hardware, this also appears as the physical emulation bottleneck: the gap between generating syntax and proving physical realizability. While software simulation is too slow for full-system physical verification, hardware emulation is extremely costly and complex to set up. This often leaves agents stranded between inaccurate software proxies and inaccessible physical ground truth.

An AI agent might propose a mathematically elegant instruction extension that passes behavioral simulation as valid text, only to fail Logic Equivalence Checking (LEC), violate DRC rules, miss PPA targets, or create a large thermal hotspot in the physical silicon. We must identify these physical limits, account for memory movement, compare tradeoffs numerically, and decide what evidence justifies moving an AI's proposed candidate out of simulation and into the physical design flow.

When AI participates, it should present a concise rationale connecting its proposals, tool results, and rejected alternatives to its final recommendation. This rationale is not evidence by itself, as @sec-feedback-verification-trust details the criteria making an observation relevant to a bounded claim. Instead, a rationale gives us a structured account to inspect for missing assumptions, contradictions, or conclusions unsupported by the measurements.

Safety engineering offers a useful precedent: later decisions are checked against the assumptions, hazards, and evidence on which they originally depended [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. If a subsequent workload shift changes the locality behavior, we can trace the rationale to see exactly why the earlier conclusion may no longer apply. While these practices make individual projects easier to review, the larger question is whether they can support AI across a much greater share of architecture design. Achieving that structural shift requires a target beyond incremental tool upgrades.

## The Lighthouse Prompt and the Architecture Moonshot {#sec-intent-vs-spec}

Engineers use the term *moonshot* for an ambitious goal requiring more than incremental improvement. X, the Moonshot Factory, describes a moonshot as the intersection of a massive problem, a radical solution, and a technological breakthrough making progress plausible [@XCompanyMoonshotBlueprint]. To organize our investigation, we introduce the **Lighthouse Prompt**, an ambitious target that spans the entire stack.

![**From design prompt to architecture study.** The prompt implies architecture requirements and engineering work that must be completed before an architect can formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend an answer.](images/F1-moonshot-stack){#fig-moonshot-prompt width="85%" fig-alt="Stacked diagram with the full Lighthouse prompt at the top, architecture requirements in the middle, and the execution and analysis requirements needed to support an answer at the bottom."}

While the full solution to the moonshot may remain distant, the target must be specific enough to organize our experiments and highlight which advances actually matter.

Computer architecture has comparable precedents. The Mead and Conway VLSI methodology[^fn-vlsi-complexity-c01] joined abstractions and design rules to tools, fabrication access, and pedagogy [@MeadConway1980VLSI]. The reduced-instruction-set program later reshaped the hardware and software contract around quantitative evidence [@PattersonDitzel1980RISC]. Because both efforts produced new methods and sharable artifacts rather than single designs, other groups could build on and teach them rather than starting from scratch.

[^fn-vlsi-complexity-c01]: **VLSI complexity (origin)**: Early-1980s frontier microprocessors held about $10^4$ to $10^5$ transistors, and by 2021 frontier chips held tens of billions [@Rupp2022MicroprocessorTrendData]. Mead and Conway answered that outgrowth of transistor-level practice with higher-level abstractions, design rules, and fabrication access that a course could teach and a community could share.

> **Architecture moonshot.** An architecture moonshot is a difficult,
> long-term target that existing tools and design practice cannot yet
> address end to end. It seeks a major improvement in how we
> use AI to formulate problems, explore and implement alternatives, evaluate
> and explain results, and defend decisions. Advances in AI, data, or tools
> must make at least part of the target practical to investigate without
> implying that the whole problem has been solved.

@fig-moonshot-venn separates the three conditions in this definition. The grand challenge represents complex architecture work that current ad hoc coordination cannot handle effectively. The radical target envisions a rigorous AI-assisted practice changing how we formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend designs. The enabling breakthrough is the convergence of AI, data, and tools making part of that target practical to investigate today. Because none of the three conditions is sufficient alone, their intersection identifies a problem worth solving, a novel approach, and a practical starting point for testing—even if the complete goal remains out of reach.

![**Three conditions for an architecture moonshot.** The target combines a major architecture challenge, a substantial change in AI-assisted design work, and an advance in AI, data, or tools that makes part of the problem practical to study.](images/F1c-moonshot-venn){#fig-moonshot-venn width="85%" fig-alt="Three-circle Venn diagram showing an architecture moonshot at the intersection of a grand challenge, a radical practice target, and an enabling breakthrough."}

Later chapters examine bounded parts of this target rather than claiming to deliver it whole.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

The three conditions do not carry equal risk. An architect can state a grand challenge and a radical target at the outset, but the enabling breakthrough is a claim about capability that has to hold in practice. A program that assumes that breakthrough rather than demonstrating it, and that names no result that would show the assumption wrong, can pursue the target for years on a premise that never arrives.

::: {.callout-war-story title="The compiler breakthrough EPIC assumed never arrived"}
**The claim.** In the 1990s, Intel and HP bet a processor generation on Explicitly Parallel Instruction Computing (EPIC), the architecture behind Itanium. EPIC moved the work of scheduling instructions from the hardware to the compiler, exposing parallelism at compile time and promising a step-change in performance over the out-of-order designs of the day [@SchlanskerRau2000EPIC].

**The gap.** That payoff depended on a sufficiently smart compiler able to find and schedule the parallelism statically. For behavior known only at run time, such as memory latency and branch outcomes, that compiler never arrived. Out-of-order superscalar processors and the AMD64 extension, announced in 1999, specified in 2000, and shipping around 2003, delivered the gains on existing software instead, and the first Itanium silicon reached the market as Merced only in 2001 [@AMD64History].

**The lesson.** A moonshot's three conditions are not equally safe to assume. The grand challenge and the radical target were sound; the program failed on the enabling breakthrough, which it assumed rather than demonstrated and paired with no result that would have shown the assumption wrong. An enabling breakthrough taken on faith, with nothing that could falsify it, is the trap. Current AI-assisted architecture risks a similar trap if it assumes LLMs can generate physically realizable microarchitectures without a closed-loop EDA toolchain to objectively verify them. An evaluation loop can catch errors, but if the generator fundamentally cannot learn physical constraints, the closed loop will simply reject every proposal without ever converging on a working design.
:::

The Lighthouse prompt, asking for a low-power 64-bit RISC-V compute subsystem for mobile XR, fits all three conditions. Today, architects manually coordinate decisions across workloads, software, hardware, tools, and physical design. The long-term aim is for AI to take on more of that search, while the architect stays answerable for the claim. Advances in models, data, and tools make bounded parts of this approach practical now.

Tool-using design-space agents already propose configurations, invoke simulators, and revise searches on the returned results, and learning-based placement already turns a netlist into a macro layout, each making one bounded slice of the loop tractable to study rather than the whole. This enabling leg is nonetheless the one most easily assumed rather than demonstrated. The Lighthouse target therefore carries an explicit stop condition. It is failing if, under matched baselines and budgets, AI carries no step of this work that conventional methods could not already do as well and more cheaply. It succeeds when an AI-assisted loop produces a design-space report for the XR subsystem whose recommendation an independent architect can verify through an evaluation harness at a lower total cost than a human-orchestrated baseline.

To apply that ambition to the Lighthouse prompt, we must clearly define *architecture* and *architect*. Architecture is more than a microarchitecture, block diagram, or chip artifact.

> **Architecture.** Architecture is the hardware and software contract and system organization turning workload intent and technology constraints into a defensible system design. It includes the instruction set architecture (ISA), microarchitecture, memory, interconnects, accelerators, chiplets, compiler interfaces, physical-design constraints, verification plans, deployment models, and the evidence used to justify choices.

> **Architect.** The architect is the owner of the decision. We frame the problem, choose abstractions, set the evidence standard, define what disqualifies a candidate, and remain answerable for the committed design. While one person may hold this role or a team may divide the work, the division must explicitly state who holds decision and approval authority. Methods can contribute candidates and analysis, but we remain responsible for synthesizing the results into a recommendation and defending the ultimate architectural claim.

### Early Ideation and Requirement Translation

One of the most profound shifts in Architecture 2.0 is applying AI to the very first steps of the design loop: early ideation and requirement translation. Historically, translating a product requirement ("we need a low-power AR headset") into a formal architectural specification ("3 W TDP, 64-bit RISC-V, 128-bit vector extensions, 4 MB shared L2") required weeks of manual profiling, spreadsheet modeling, and expert intuition.

AI assists in this translation by rapidly bridging the gap between natural language intent and formal constraints. An LLM can ingest product requirements, cross-reference them against historical designs and public benchmarks, and propose a parameterized design space. More importantly, it can help us formalize ambiguity. When a prompt asks for a "real-time" workload, an AI agent can query us to bound that term: *Does real-time mean a strict 16 millisecond frame deadline, or 99th-percentile tail latency?*

This early assistance shifts our focus from writing boilerplate specifications to critiquing the proposed bounds. Formalizing the constraints early ensures that downstream generation and evaluation tools are searching a mathematically well-defined space, rather than one built on unstated assumptions. The output of this phase must be a machine-readable specification, such as a parameterized Chisel generator template or a sweeping configuration file, rather than a prose document.

## Unpacking Lighthouse Architecture Questions {#sec-why-the-prompt-spans-the-stack}

To defend such a claim, we must first unpack the Lighthouse prompt into specific, verifiable architectural decisions. @fig-moonshot-prompt makes the scale of the Lighthouse prompt explicit. The prompt occupies only a few lines, but answering it requires choices about the workload, software contract, compute organization, memory, power, compiler, runtime, physical design, reliability, and verification. The bottom panel of the figure shows the work required to connect those choices to tools, evidence, and an architectural decision.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

Start with the prompt itself. High-level intent first becomes questions and coupled design choices, not a generated chip artifact. The prompt tasks us with comparing several compute organizations for a real-time mobile extended-reality (XR) workload, all while respecting a 3 W thermal-design-power (TDP) budget and the limits of a low-power (LP) process. Individual phrases bound different parts of the study.

The phrase "64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem" establishes the ISA, ABI, and software contract. The "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload" specification sets the target environment. The "accelerator block" provision leaves several compute organizations open for comparison. Finally, the 3 W target and 3 nm-class LP process supply hard physical limits that can rule out an unfeasible proposal. A 3 W TDP in a mobile XR form factor forces passive cooling, strict skin-temperature limits, dense advanced packaging, and high-bandwidth signaling under a tight power budget.

While the prompt asks for a design-space report, the study may also produce software, models, RTL, layouts, prototypes, or a physical chip—depending on the ambition of the claim. The report connects those artifacts and measurements to the proposed design and explains precisely why alternatives were rejected. To test our judgment, the prompt deliberately leaves the accelerator microarchitecture, memory system, software integration, and evaluation tools open.

@tbl-moonshot-decomposition collects the architectural decisions implied by
the Lighthouse prompt and the checks that could disqualify an answer. AI may
suggest a hardware and software decomposition. The architect accepts, revises,
or rejects that proposed scope and its coupled interfaces.

| **Prompt fragment** | **Architectural decisions** | **What must be checked** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **XRBench mobile XR** | Workload slice, input distribution, quality-of-service target, latency deadline, memory traffic, software pipeline, and drift assumptions. | Trace source, benchmark version, documented workload-phase selection, and rejection when results over-optimize a single hot loop or miss real-time behavior. |
| **64-bit RISC-V with vector or accelerator option** | ISA boundary, custom extension policy, programming model, compiler/runtime path, library support, and software compatibility. | Correctness, toolchain support, generated-code evidence, portability checks, and rejection of unsupported software semantics. |
| **Compute subsystem** | CPU, fixed-function accelerator, reconfigurable spatial array (e.g., CGRA or Reconfigurable Dataflow Architecture/RDA), or Processing-in-Memory (PIM) data-centric block; memory hierarchy (SRAM/DRAM sizing), Network-on-Chip (NoC) routing, SoC integration, interface compatibility, and relevant reliability or isolation assumptions. | Design-space comparison and rejection of candidates that only win by moving cost, bandwidth, energy, complexity, or risk elsewhere. |
| **3 W, 3 nm-class low-power envelope** | Power, voltage/frequency, thermal, area, EDA constraints, and dynamic power management interfaces (e.g., Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling or DVFS, power domains, thermal sensors) for the OS/firmware. | Source, version, and calibration of the power model; physical-design limits (IR drop, electromigration, timing closure); sensitivity analysis; and rejection criteria for runtime throttling or reliability failures within the scoped workload. |
| **Design-space report with evidence and rejected alternatives** | Alternatives, the Pareto frontier (the set of objective outcomes of feasible designs for which no alternative is at least as good on every objective and strictly better on at least one) [@Deb2001MultiObjective], assumptions, uncertainty, verification plan, rejected or failed candidates, and points at which the responsible architect decides whether to continue. | Measurements linked to assumptions and checks, rejected candidates, coverage, reproducible artifacts, and a named person responsible for the recommendation. |

: **What each prompt fragment requires.** The Lighthouse prompt maps to decisions about workload definition, software and ISA contracts, hardware organization, physical feasibility, and evidence. {#tbl-moonshot-decomposition tbl-colwidths="[18,34,35]"}

The table is a first-pass checklist rather than the final review form. An
answer is incomplete if it cannot identify its workload slice, software
contract, physical assumptions, supporting observations, or decision owner.
The rest of the book develops these requirements and turns them into a compact
review form.

Each row points to engineering depth that the short prompt leaves unstated. To verify whether a candidate actually fits the "3 W, 3 nm-class" constraint, we need results from RTL, synthesis, floorplanning, timing, IR drop, leakage, thermal analysis, and final signoff. Comparing the RISC-V vector and accelerator alternatives requires checking the compiler, runtime, libraries, generated code, and software portability. The Lighthouse prompt is therefore a computer architecture problem rather than a prompt engineering exercise, demanding an answer that connects decisions and evidence across the stack.

The workload and process phrases require more detail than the compact prompt provides. XRBench, a benchmark suite for mobile XR workloads, supplies a concrete workload anchor rather than a vague application label [@KwonEtAl2023XRBench]. End-to-end XR research shows how tightly perception and display stages are coupled, making mobile XR a system-level architecture target rather than a single kernel to optimize [@HuzaifaEtAl2021ILLIXR]. AI can help here by analyzing dataset distributions to find coverage gaps, complementing profiling, trace analysis, and expert workload judgment. For instance, an AI agent could ingest the XRBench memory trace and automatically cluster access patterns to recommend specific cache hierarchies or local scratchpads before any simulation begins.

The selected benchmark must strictly represent the system claim. Similarly, the process node is stated as a broad class rather than a named foundry process design kit (PDK). Even though current mobile SoCs are publicly described in 3 nm-class technology, the study must explicitly name the process, libraries, voltage assumptions, and signoff path [@Apple2024A18Pro; @Apple2024M4]. The AI's suggestions are then evaluated within a physical harness incorporating PPA and STA limits.

We might begin with an XRBench trace slice and software pipeline, propose vector-width or CPU and accelerator alternatives, and screen them with a cheap proxy. We can then reserve scarce compiler, simulator, RTL, or physical-design feedback for the candidates whose results could actually alter the final decision. That selection rule, along with what the resulting evidence may support, should be stated before the run.

Within that workflow, generation might produce legal vector configurations, prediction might estimate their data-movement risks, and optimization might choose which configurations merit expensive evaluation. A proxy may rank candidates one way, a simulator may overturn that ranking, and an EDA run may return timing paths and congestion rather than a single score. Search can also overfit[^fn-overfitting-c01] a convenient proxy.

[^fn-overfitting-c01]: **Overfitting**: The machine-learning failure of fitting the noise of a training sample so closely that performance does not generalize to new data. An optimizer can overfit a proxy the same way, raising the measured score by exploiting the proxy's quirks rather than improving the true objective.

Search can also overfit a convenient proxy, or worse, commit *silicon overfitting*. Because software models evolve in weeks while silicon takes three years to fabricate, an AI agent cannot simply hyperspecialize a 3nm accelerator for today's static trace. The architect must force the AI to balance extreme specialization with enough programmability to survive the fabrication time lag. 

While these methods narrow the work, their output alone does not prove whether a compiler assumption holds, whether the measured behavior follows from the proposed mechanism, or whether the remaining tradeoff justifies a multimillion-dollar commitment. The final evidence should document the candidates, assumptions, measurements, and rejections that affect the claim without needing every internal model trace. We must resolve the remaining questions using this curated body of evidence.

## Formulating Efficiency Claims {#sec-efficiency-claims-need-rejectable-loops}

Even if an AI-assisted process can produce a candidate and report end to end, the efficiency claim still requires a scoped system comparison. The difference between a plausible candidate and a defensible design is especially clear in an efficiency claim, because the Lighthouse subsystem cannot be judged from one score. Workload behavior, software support, data movement, physical limits, verification effort, and deployment conditions can all change which design is preferable. AI may help us coordinate those choices, but the resulting claim still demands evidence for the stated workload and constraints.

Efficiency is a strict claim about how well a particular architecture performs useful work with limited resources. The claim must name the exact design, workload, and resource budget it covers. A faster design fails to solve the problem if it exceeds the power budget, cannot be verified, or depends on fragile software assumptions.

Efficiency now spans far more than performance and power. Classical computer architecture made performance quantitative while treating cost and power as first-class constraints [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Dennard-style scaling once made it easier to improve performance while keeping power density manageable [@DennardEtAl1974Scaling]. When that scaling regime weakened, dark silicon (the fraction of a chip that must stay powered off to fit its thermal budget) and the limits of multicore scaling pushed the field toward specialization [@BorkarChien2011FutureMicroprocessors; @EsmaeilzadehEtAl2011DarkSilicon; @HennessyPatterson2019GoldenAge].

The energy cost of data movement soon made arithmetic alone an incomplete measure [@Horowitz2014Energy]. Warehouse-scale operation expanded the boundary to include power delivery, utilization, networking, operations, and total cost of ownership [@BarrosoHolzleRanganathan2019DatacenterAsComputer]. Today, sustainability adds operational and embodied carbon to the comparison, factors that depend heavily on energy use, hardware manufacturing, utilization, geography, and system lifetime [@GuptaEtAl2021ChasingCarbon]. An AI-assisted comparison must clearly state the assumptions and evidence relevant to each efficiency dimension and explicitly name the violations, such as DRC failures or thermal limits, that would immediately rule out a candidate during the evaluation harness review.

A valid comparison must also account for failed and rejected alternatives, not
only the winners. Keeping those results prevents later architects from
reevaluating candidates that earlier evidence ruled out. It also shows
which parts of the design space the claim actually covers.

Modern benchmark suites already reflect this broader view. MLPerf, a community
effort to make machine-learning performance claims reproducible across
systems, includes deployment scenario, latency, throughput, and accuracy in its
comparisons [@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. Useful work must be evaluated
against the resource that is scarce in the stated setting, whether that is
latency, throughput, energy, power, area, cost, carbon, reliability,
verification effort, engineering time, or risk.

The design, workload, scenario, and scarce resource together define what
"efficient" means. For the Lighthouse system, a candidate that improves average
throughput may still be a poor choice if it misses an XR frame deadline, exceeds
the 3 W envelope under sustained load, or adds verification work that the
project cannot absorb. Changing any of those conditions changes the claim and
the evidence needed to support it.

@tbl-efficiency-dimensions separates six dimensions that an architect may need
to compare. The architect should select the dimensions that define the claim.
Reading the rows together also shows where a gain may shift cost or risk
elsewhere.

| **Dimension** | **Efficiency question** | **Why it complicates the comparison** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Performance** | How much useful work is delivered per unit time, latency budget, or service-level target? | The answer depends on workload selection, scenario, software stack, and whether the measured behavior matches the deployment claim. |
| **Power and energy** | How much useful work is delivered per watt, joule, thermal budget, or battery envelope? | A credible comparison must account for activity, data movement, voltage/frequency choices, thermal constraints, and fidelity gaps between estimates and signoff. |
| **Reliability and correctness** | How much useful work survives faults, corner cases, nondeterminism, and validation? | A faster candidate is not efficient if it spends its savings on fragility, security side-channels, debug burden, or invalid software and hardware assumptions. |
| **Scalability and cost** | How much useful work is delivered per dollar, rack, network hop, operator action, or unit of capacity? | Local wins can shift cost to memory, network, power delivery, utilization, operations, or total cost of ownership. |
| **Sustainability** | How much useful work is delivered per unit of operational and embodied environmental footprint? | Carbon depends on hardware lifetime, manufacturing, energy mix, utilization, and where and when computation runs. |
| **Evidence and engineering effort** | How much decision-relevant evidence is obtained per simulation, experiment, verification run, or engineer-hour? | Generating more candidates can still be inefficient if evaluation consumes scarce feedback, hides failures, or cannot reject weak outputs. |

: **Dimensions of an efficiency claim.** Performance, power, reliability, scalability, sustainability, and evidence cost are increasingly coupled. A valid comparison must show when an apparent improvement shifts cost or changes the scope. {#tbl-efficiency-dimensions tbl-colwidths="[22,32,34]"}

Two kinds of efficiency operate in this comparison. The proposed hardware system must perform useful work within its resource limits, and the study itself must allocate simulator runs, physical-design experiments, verification effort, and expert review carefully enough to reach a reliable conclusion. Reducing the cost of the study is valuable only when the remaining observations can still distinguish the alternatives and expose the failures that matter to the decision.

These efficiency dimensions interact. In the mobile XR setting anchored by XRBench and ILLIXR, frame latency, energy, sustained power, and data movement are so tightly coupled that a gain in one can violate a deadline or thermal limit in another. Better simulated performance can be offset by added verification work, and lower energy may depend on fragile software assumptions. A candidate that wins under a proxy may fail on a more faithful workload. We must explain which tradeoffs are acceptable for the stated constraints, defining the scope of the efficiency claim instead of relying on a single attractive artifact or score.

These coupled requirements do not imply that every architecture project needs AI. Traditional architecture methods remain effective when the workload, abstraction, feedback path, and proposed change are clearly bounded. We should first identify what familiar models, scripts, simulators, and expert reviews already handle well before stating exactly what we expect AI to improve. The test is whether the assistance leads to a better system or a more reliable conclusion, rather than simply generating more artifacts.

A common objection is that today's models hallucinate and cannot be trusted to produce correct hardware, so an AI-assisted architecture program is premature. The objection is right about reliability and wrong about what the program needs. Nothing here asks a model to be trustworthy on its own. It asks that every AI proposal pass the same rejection checks, matched baselines, and evidence standard as any other candidate, so that an unreliable generator is bounded by the loop rather than believed. The open question is not whether models are reliable enough to trust, but whether the loop around them can catch their errors cheaply enough to be worth running. If every hallucinatory proposal requires a multi-hour synthesis run to reject, the computational cost of the evaluation loop will rapidly eclipse the human engineering time it was meant to save.

The Lighthouse prompt makes this friction concrete. The requested subsystem must support a workload class, meet a strict power envelope, fit a technology assumption, interact correctly with software, and produce reviewable evidence. The final comparison must weigh energy, latency, memory traffic, programmability, verification, and deployment risk. While a single scalar objective may help an automated search navigate the space, it cannot substitute for architectural judgment.

```{=latex}
\Needspace{20\baselineskip}
```

## Evaluating Existing Systems

Applying this judgment to today's tools reveals both rapid progress and persistent gaps. Current work has demonstrated useful parts of AI-assisted architecture under
bounded conditions. Predictive design-space exploration uses simulated performance to train surrogate models that estimate behavior across a specified design space [@IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. AutoTVM uses measured hardware execution to guide a statistical search for tensor-program schedules [@ChenEtAl2018AutoTVM]. In each case, the input, architecture job, feedback source, and bounded result remain clear.

AlphaChip, a learning-based macro-placement method, is a prominent physical-design example. Its inputs are a netlist and macro descriptions, its job is macro placement, and its feedback comes from proxy rewards such as wirelength and congestion [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement]. Its reported placement speed established how fast the method produces a candidate, not that it beats strong classical baselines, and its reported deployment across several TPU generations is relevant evidence without settling that matched comparison. @sec-feedback-verification-trust examines the proxy-metric and baseline dispute in full.

Generation methods have begun to reach real hardware in narrow settings. In a conversational study, a language model drafted the Verilog for a small 8-bit processor that reached tapeout, though a human guided the model through many revisions [@BlockloveEtAl2023ChipChat]. A separate method that learns a CPU's logic from input-output examples produced a RISC-V core, Enlightenment-1, that was fabricated and runs Linux, reaching that result through a non-agentic search rather than a tool-using loop [@ChengEtAl2024AutomatedCPU]. Each reaches silicon on a narrow slice of the problem, and no autonomous loop has yet carried a full architecture study from intent to fabricated hardware.

These tools succeed at their individual tasks while exposing the integration problem that a whole architecture study must solve. Adjacent fields faced a version of it and moved forward by adopting shared baselines, standardized datasets, and common evaluation. ImageNet [@RussakovskyEtAl2015ImageNet] gave computer vision a common benchmark, and CASP (Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction) [@MoultEtAl1995CASP] let AlphaFold's [@JumperEtAl2021AlphaFold] structure predictions be measured against community-held standards. Computer architecture needs the same shared tasks, public datasets, and open evaluation protocols to turn narrow tool use into a reviewable AI-assisted practice.

Progress toward the moonshot will require more than isolated demonstrations. In software engineering, benchmarks like SWE-bench, a benchmark of real GitHub-issue fixes, provide immediate, exact correctness checks via unit tests, and agents have advanced quickly on it (@fig-swebench-progress) [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench]. Hardware benchmarking differs because evaluation is delayed and physical limits are non-linear. An AI agent might output structurally valid RTL, but that code must eventually satisfy coupled physical silicon constraints like timing closure, design rules, and thermal budgets. Correctness requires passing through many siloed EDA tools.

```{python}
#| label: fig-swebench-progress
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Coding agents now resolve most of SWE-bench Verified.** Best reported SWE-bench Verified score [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench], one point per released model, tracked through Epoch AI's benchmarking hub [@EpochAI2025AIModels]; the line traces the best result to date. Scores climbed from roughly a third of this human-filtered 500-task subset to over four-fifths in about seventeen months. Hardware has no comparably fast, exact feedback loop, which is why architecture cannot simply copy this trajectory.
#| out-width: "88%"
#| fig-alt: "Scatter of SWE-bench Verified resolved percentage versus model release date from late 2024 to 2026. Points climb from about 31 percent to about 84 percent, with a line tracing the best-to-date frontier through GPT-4o, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Claude Opus 4, GPT-5, and Claude Opus 4.7."

import csv
import datetime
from pathlib import Path

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import _python.arch2_plots as _ap
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style
from _python.labels import place_labels

apply_style()

# Data: SWE-bench Verified results [@JimenezEtAl2024SWEBench] tracked in Epoch
# AI's benchmarking hub [@EpochAI2025AIModels]; best score per released model.
# Values dataset-transcribed. Accessed 2026-07-19.
# Receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter1-swebench-verified.csv
_root = Path(_ap.__file__).resolve().parents[2]

def _yfrac(text):
    for fmt in ("%Y-%m-%d", "%Y-%m", "%Y"):
        try:
            d = datetime.datetime.strptime(text.strip(), fmt)
            return d.year + (d.timetuple().tm_yday - 1) / 365.25
        except ValueError:
            continue
    return None

pts = []
with open(_root / "data" / "source-receipts" / "chapter1-swebench-verified.csv", encoding="utf-8") as fh:
    for row in csv.DictReader(fh):
        x = _yfrac(row["release_date"])
        try:
            score = float(row["resolved_fraction"])
        except ValueError:
            continue
        if x is None:
            continue
        pts.append({"model": row["model"], "score": score * 100 if score <= 1 else score, "x": x})
pts.sort(key=lambda p: p["x"])
best = -1.0
frontier = []
for p in pts:
    if p["score"] > best:
        best = p["score"]
        frontier.append(p)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.4, 3.2))
ax.scatter([p["x"] for p in pts], [p["score"] for p in pts], s=16, marker="o",
           facecolors="white", edgecolors=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.7, zorder=2, label="Model releases")
ax.plot([p["x"] for p in frontier], [p["score"] for p in frontier], color=COLORS["evidence"], lw=1.6, zorder=3)
ax.scatter([p["x"] for p in frontier], [p["score"] for p in frontier], s=22, marker="o",
           facecolor=COLORS["evidence"], edgecolor=COLORS["evidence_ink"], linewidth=0.5, zorder=4, label="Best to date")
ax.set_xlim(2024.8, 2026.55)
ax.set_ylim(20, 92)
ax.set_xlabel("Model release date", fontsize=7.2)
ax.set_ylabel("SWE-bench Verified resolved (%)", fontsize=7.2)
ax.set_xticks([2025.0, 2025.5, 2026.0, 2026.5])
ax.set_xticklabels(["2025", "mid-25", "2026", "mid-26"], fontsize=6.4)
ax.tick_params(labelsize=6.4, length=2.5, width=0.6)
for _sp in ("top", "right"):
    ax.spines[_sp].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(True, which="major", axis="y", color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.4, zorder=0)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", frameon=False, fontsize=6.0, handletextpad=0.3, labelspacing=0.3)
want = ["GPT-4o", "Claude 3.7 Sonnet", "Claude Opus 4", "GPT-5", "Claude Opus 4.7"]
by_name = {}
for p in frontier:
    key = (p["model"] or "").strip()
    if key in want and key not in by_name:
        by_name[key] = p
items = [(by_name[w]["x"], by_name[w]["score"], w) for w in want if w in by_name]
place_labels(ax, fig, items, fontsize=5.0, color=COLORS["ink"])
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.10, right=0.97, top=0.98, bottom=0.12)
```

The climb is what a fast, exact feedback loop buys. Best reported scores rose from roughly a third of real issues to over four-fifths in about seventeen months, because every attempt is graded in seconds against unit tests. Architecture has no equivalent loop because physical constraints like PPA, STA, DRC rules, and thermal budgets require heavy, slow EDA simulations. This is why its own progress has instead depended on shared tasks, inspectable tools, and the human architect carefully structuring the evaluation harness. To achieve SWE-bench-like iteration speeds, the architecture community must develop intermediate proxies—such as graph-based structural checkers or fast static analysis—that can reject obvious failures in seconds without requiring full synthesis.

SPEC-style benchmarks made performance claims more comparable by specifying workloads, run rules, and reporting standards. Shared simulators such as gem5 made architecture experiments easier to inspect across labs. FPGA-accelerated simulation platforms such as FireSim, community benchmark suites such as MLPerf, artifact-evaluation practices, and open tool flows succeeded because they let researchers understand, rerun, challenge, and safely build on reported results [@SPEC2017CPU; @BinkertEtAl2011Gem5; @KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim; @MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. AI-assisted results will need the same shared tasks, tool interfaces, and evidence standards to matter beyond a single study.

### Why Are We Still Writing This Book?

If these components are so promising, why is AI-assisted architecture not a solved problem? The answer lies in data scarcity and the massive verification load.

Software and hardware diverge sharply on the data axis. A coding agent can observe millions of open-source pull requests, Stack Overflow discussions, and unit tests to learn idiomatic Python or C++. In contrast, high-fidelity hardware IP, process design kits (PDKs), and production silicon design data are proprietary, closely held, and rarely published. With little open RTL, cycle-accurate memory traces, or production floorplans available in its pretraining corpus, a foundation model cannot magically acquire an understanding of power-delivery routing or 3 nm timing constraints. When it attempts to generate hardware structures zero-shot, it relies on superficial textual patterns rather than physical invariants, often proposing designs that look correct but are fundamentally unroutable or physically impossible to cool.

Because the model lacks this latent knowledge, it must learn these constraints interactively during the design loop. It submits a candidate to a toolchain, encounters a DRC error or timing violation, and attempts to revise the design. However, this feedback loop is orders of magnitude slower than a software unit test. This structural data deficit explains why open hardware ecosystems like RISC-V and OpenROAD are so critical. They provide the accessible bootstrap data needed to train architecture models, serving as the only viable bridge into the synthesis and physical-design loops that Architecture 2.0 must demonstrate. However, even these open ecosystems currently offer only hundreds of high-quality designs—orders of magnitude fewer than the millions of software repositories used to train coding agents—requiring researchers to develop fundamentally more sample-efficient learning methods.

Scarce data also raises the verification stakes. A model that cannot generate correct hardware zero-shot[^fn-zero-shot-c01] emits plausible but subtly flawed structures, and verification already eats over half of a project's engineering effort. Producing candidates faster than reviewers can check them makes that worse, so driving formal-verification tools and constrained-random testing is part of the work, not an afterthought. A generated cache controller that hides a coherence deadlock is worse than useless if the deadlock takes months to find.

[^fn-zero-shot-c01]: **Zero-shot generation**: Producing a correct output for a task the model has seen no examples of, on the first attempt and without feedback. A model that cannot do this for RTL must learn through tool feedback instead, which places the slow EDA toolchain inside its training loop.

Verification must also reach past functional correctness to hardware security. After Spectre [@Kocher2019Spectre], a loop that emits hardware has to check its own structures for microarchitectural side channels, isolation-boundary violations, and hardware Trojans, which means generating the SystemVerilog assertions, security properties, and testbenches those checks require.

There is much more to come, and that is why we are writing this book. The rest of the book develops the representations, tool interfaces, methods, evidence practices, and decision rules this chapter has named:

- @sec-data-representations-world-models explains architectural knowledge, current design state, and predicted consequences.
- @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces explains legal multi-tool action and typed feedback.
- @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization explains AI roles and component organization.
- @sec-feedback-verification-trust explains evidence, challenge, and decision relevance.
- @sec-running-the-loop shows one bounded integrated study.
- @sec-evaluating-agentic-architect examines how we benchmark these agents and bounds their reasoning capacity.
- @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack shows what changes across feedback conditions and time.
- @sec-what-architect-owns synthesizes architect responsibility.

@sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping shows how to bootstrap one bounded study, @sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card gives a compact way to inspect a completed study, and @sec-appendix-c-reviewer-checklist provides a reviewer's checklist.

## Open Research Questions

Every chapter in this book ends here, with the questions its argument leaves open. The sections are not a summary of what was settled. They mark where the work runs out, so a reader who wants to push on the subject knows where the edges are.

Several questions stay open, and progress on the moonshot depends on them more than on any single demonstration. The hardest ones concern the design data the field cannot access, the fast feedback loop hardware cannot yet run, and the standard that would let an autonomous study count as real architecture work.

**Theme 1: The Bootstrap Data Wall.** Software models learn from millions of open pull requests and unit tests, while high-fidelity hardware data stays proprietary and closely held.

- How can a model learn foundry-calibrated physical-design behavior, such as power-delivery routing or 3 nm-class timing limits, without access to the proprietary PDKs that encode it?
- How far can open ecosystems like RISC-V and OpenROAD substitute for closed design data as a bootstrap corpus, and where does the gap to production silicon remain?

**Theme 2: The Missing Fast Loop.** Software agents improve fast because every attempt is graded in seconds; hardware has no comparably fast, exact feedback loop.

- What evaluation harness could give a design loop feedback fast and exact enough to drive real learning, given that PPA, STA, DRC, and thermal checks each demand slow EDA runs?
- Under matched baselines and budgets, how cheaply can a rejectable loop catch a generator's errors, such as a hidden coherence deadlock or a microarchitectural side channel, for the loop to be worth running rather than the generator trusted?
- How much of the verification load, including SystemVerilog assertions, security properties, and coverage models, can an AI loop produce to a standard a sign-off engineer would accept?

**Theme 3: Defining the Win.** The Lighthouse target carries an explicit stop condition, yet judging a completed run against it is itself unsettled.

- When does automated search over these non-convex design spaces actually exceed what a human team could direct, rather than produce a larger set of candidates?
- What would let an autonomous run from workload characterization to timing-closed layout be judged a real architecture study rather than an interpolation of known baselines, and how would a reviewer separate genuine architectural abstraction from a design memorized from nearby topologies? The field must define explicit novelty metrics—such as demonstrating non-obvious microarchitectural compositions or discovering Pareto-optimal points outside the training distribution—to prove the agent is synthesizing rather than simply retrieving.

## Conclusion

::: {.callout-design-principle title="Non-Delegable Responsibility"}
Automation can synthesize candidates, but structural integrity remains human work. The architect delineates boundaries, defines objectives, and holds ultimate accountability for physical invariants. Delegating the search does not delegate the validation.
:::


The Lighthouse prompt shows what it would take for AI to help an architect formulate, explore, implement, evaluate, explain, and defend a system design with evidence rather than settle for a plausible candidate. A defensible answer needs a clear question, meaningful alternatives, checkable artifacts, experiments, a mechanism-level explanation, and a recommendation. Readers should judge the whole body of work, not only the artifact AI produced, and ask whether its evidence supports the architectural claim.

Efficiency now couples performance, energy, power delivery, reliability, sustainability, cost, verification effort, and the time needed to obtain useful evidence. When plausible artifacts are cheap, evaluation often becomes the expensive part of the work. Search, tool feedback, interpretation, evidence, or review can each turn out to be the limiting resource.

AI models and tool interfaces are changing quickly, and how far they can carry architecture work is still unsettled. What does not change is where responsibility sits. We frame the task, choose abstractions, representations, and tools, set the evidence standard, interpret the mechanisms, and remain answerable for the committed design. Keeping the inputs, results, rejections, and decisions lets others challenge our work and improve it.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Evaluate AI across the complete architecture work it claims to assist. A generated artifact matters only in relation to the question, alternatives, implementation path, evaluation, explanation, and decision it advances.
- **Reader test:** Can a reader take the Lighthouse request, identify its workload, software, hardware, memory, physical, verification, and decision questions, and state which parts current AI systems have and have not demonstrated?
- **Up next:** @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales explains why the architecture work introduced here has become harder to complete as specialization, software change, evaluation cost, and cross-layer coordination reinforce one another.
:::
